title: Rounded Percentage Calculation
description: Given an integer `amount` and an integer `gstPercent` (0..28), return `amount * gstPercent / 100` rounded to the nearest integer using round-half-away-from-zero. Both inputs are non-negative. Use integer arithmetic for the final rounding rather than floating-point, to avoid precision errors.
hints: Multiply first, then divide — agar pehle divide karega toh precision khone ka risk.
Round-half-away: (numerator + 50) / 100 with integer division when non-negative.
amount aur percent dono non-negative hain, simple.

title: Quotient and Remainder Split
description: Given an integer `lakhs`, return an array `[crores, remainingLakhs]` such that `lakhs = crores * 100 + remainingLakhs` and `0 <= remainingLakhs < 100`. If `lakhs` is negative, treat it as invalid and return `[0, 0]`.
hints: Math.floor(lakhs/100) gives crores; lakhs % 100 gives remaining.
Negative case ko alag handle kar.
Integer division — don't be tempted by ` / `.

title: Plus One
description: You are given a non-negative integer represented as an array of decimal digits `digits`, where the most significant digit is at the front and each element is in the range [0, 9]. The integer has no leading zeros except when it is `0` itself. Increment the integer by one and return the resulting array of digits.
hints: Right-to-left carry propagation.
Agar last digit < 9 toh +1 karke return.
Sab 9 ho toh aage ek nayi 1 prepend karna padta hai.

title: Happy Number
description: A positive integer is called happy if repeatedly replacing it with the sum of the squares of its decimal digits eventually reaches `1`; otherwise the process loops endlessly in a cycle that never includes `1`. Given a positive integer `n`, return `true` if `n` is a happy number, and `false` otherwise.
hints: Helper: digit square sum.
Set me visit kar; agar 1 mila → true, agar duplicate aaya → false.
Floyd ka cycle detection bhi chalega — slow & fast pointer.

title: Palindrome Number
description: Given an integer `x`, return `true` if `x` reads the same forward and backward, and `false` otherwise. Negative numbers are not palindromes because of the leading sign. Solve this without converting the integer to a string.

examples.explanation: Reads as -121 vs 121-; not a palindrome.

hints: Negative ya non-zero ending in 0 → false (except 0 itself).
Half-reverse trick: build reverse of bottom half, stop when reversed >= remaining.
Match: top == reversed OR top == reversed/10 (odd-length case).

title: Reverse Integer
description: Given a signed 32-bit integer `x`, return `x` with its decimal digits reversed, preserving the sign. If reversing `x` causes the result to fall outside the signed 32-bit range [-2^31, 2^31 - 1], return `0`.
hints: Pop digits with mod, push to result with multiply-add.
Pre-overflow check: result kabhi (INT_MAX-d)/10 se zyada nahi hona chahiye.
Negative sign khud manage karta hai mod operator JS me, but check carefully.

title: Pow(x, n)
description: Implement `pow(x, n)`, which raises `x` (a floating-point number) to the integer power `n`, without using a built-in power function. For negative `n`, the result is `1 / pow(x, -n)`. Return the result as a number.
hints: Iterative binary exponentiation: while n>0, if n odd multiply result.
Handle negative n separately (compute positive power, invert).
INT_MIN ka special case — use BigInt or convert carefully.

title: Sqrt(x)
description: Given a non-negative integer `x`, return the integer square root of `x`, i.e. `floor(sqrt(x))`. You must not use any built-in square-root or exponentiation function; compute the answer using binary search.
hints: Binary search for the largest k such that k*k <= x.
Search range: [0, x]. Early prune: x >= 2 → upper bound x/2 + 1.
Watch overflow when multiplying mid*mid for very large x — use BigInt or compare via division.

title: Divide Two Integers
description: Given two integers `dividend` and `divisor`, return the quotient of dividing `dividend` by `divisor` without using multiplication, division, or the modulo operator. The division truncates toward zero. The result must fit in a signed 32-bit integer; if it would exceed `2^31 - 1`, return `2^31 - 1`, and if it would be less than `-2^31`, return `-2^31`. The divisor is never zero.
hints: Sign nikaal aur dono ko absolute kar.
Doubling-divisor trick: subtract largest 2^k * divisor that fits.
Overflow check at the end.

title: Roman to Integer
description: Given a string `s` representing a valid Roman numeral in the range 1 to 3999, convert it to its integer value. Roman numerals are normally written from largest to smallest left to right; when a smaller numeral precedes a larger one, it is subtracted (for example, `IV` is 4 and `IX` is 9).
constraints: s consists of I, V, X, L, C, D, M only
s represents a valid Roman numeral 1..3999
hints: Map each char to value.
Iterate left→right; if current < next, subtract; else add.
Last char always added.

title: Excel Sheet Column Title
description: Given a positive integer `n`, return its corresponding column title as it appears in a spreadsheet, where `1 -> "A"`, `2 -> "B"`, ..., `26 -> "Z"`, `27 -> "AA"`, `28 -> "AB"`, and so on. This is a bijective base-26 system with no zero digit, so subtract one before taking each digit.
hints: Bijective base 26 — no zero digit. So before mod, do n-=1.
Loop: char = (n-1) % 26 + 'A', then n = (n-1)/26.
Build chars in reverse, then reverse string.

title: Count Primes
description: Given a non-negative integer `n`, return the number of prime numbers that are strictly less than `n`.
hints: Boolean array of length n; mark composites.
Outer loop i from 2 to sqrt(n); for each prime, mark multiples starting at i*i.
Count remaining trues from 2 to n-1.

title: Greatest Common Divisor
description: Given two non-negative integers `a` and `b`, return their greatest common divisor (GCD) using the Euclidean algorithm. By convention, `gcd(0, 0) = 0`.
hints: gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, a%b).
Base: b == 0 → return a.
Iterative > recursive for huge inputs.

title: Spiral Matrix
description: Given an `m x n` matrix `matrix`, return all of its elements in spiral order, traversing the layers from the outside inward (right across the top, down the right side, left across the bottom, then up the left side). Return the result as a flat array.
hints: Maintain four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right.
Walk one direction, shrink the corresponding boundary, repeat.
Stop when boundaries cross.

title: Rotate Image
description: You are given an `n x n` matrix `matrix` representing an image. Rotate the image 90 degrees clockwise in place, modifying the input directly without allocating another matrix, and return the same matrix reference.
hints: Trick: transpose, then reverse each row.
Transpose: swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i] for i<j.
In-place — extra matrix banaane ki zaroorat nahi.

title: Set Matrix Zeroes
description: Given an `m x n` integer matrix `matrix`, if an element is `0`, set its entire row and its entire column to `0`. You must do this in place, using only constant extra space beyond the input, and return the modified matrix.
hints: Use first row & first column themselves as marker arrays.
Track separately whether row 0 / col 0 originally contained a zero.
Final pass: zero out cells based on markers; finally zero row 0 / col 0 if needed.

title: Minimum Time Visiting All Points
description: Given an array `points` where each `points[i] = [x, y]` is an integer coordinate, return the minimum total number of unit steps to visit all points in the given order. In one step you may move one unit horizontally, vertically, or diagonally, so the minimum cost between two consecutive points is the Chebyshev distance `max(|dx|, |dy|)`.
hints: 8-direction grid → Chebyshev distance = max(|dx|, |dy|).
Sum over consecutive pairs.
Single point → 0.

title: Multiply Strings
description: Given two non-negative integers `num1` and `num2` represented as strings, return their product, also as a string. Neither string has leading zeros except `"0"` itself. You must not convert the inputs directly to a numeric type or use any big-integer library; perform the multiplication digit by digit.
constraints: num1 and num2 consist of digits only
Neither has leading zeros except '0' itself
hints: Allocate result array of size m+n; digit-by-digit multiplication.
Position rule: num1[i] * num2[j] contributes to result[i+j+1] with carry to result[i+j].
Skip leading zeros in final string; '0' edge case.

title: Fraction to Recurring Decimal
description: Given two integers `numerator` and `denominator` (with `denominator != 0`), return the value of the fraction `numerator / denominator` as a string. If the fractional part is repeating, enclose the repeating block in parentheses (for example, `1/3` becomes `"0.(3)"` and `4/333` becomes `"0.(012)"`). If multiple answers are possible, return any valid one.
hints: Sign determine kar, fir absolute values pe long division.
Map<remainder, indexInResult> — repeat remainder mile toh yahin loop start.
Watch INT_MIN; absolute value safe rakhne ke liye Number ya BigInt sambhal.

title: Permutation Sequence
description: The set `[1, 2, ..., n]` has `n!` distinct permutations. Listing them in lexicographic order, return the `k`-th permutation (1-indexed) as a string of digits. Compute the answer directly using factorials rather than generating all permutations.
hints: Determine each digit by quotient with (n-1)! block size.
Maintain available-digit list; remove the chosen one each step.
0-index k internally (k -= 1) to make math clean.
